analogy: Think of "Force and Laws of Motion" through the chapter's own opening images: Atomic Foundations of Matter Chapter 9 In Chapter 8, Journey Inside the Atom, you explored the structure of the atom. You also studied subatomic particles, viz., electrons, protons and neutrons, in terms of their discoveries, properties and locations in the atom. You also learnt that... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Force and Laws of Motion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that atomic Foundations of Matter Chapter 9 In Chapter 8, Journey Inside the Atom, you explored the structure of the atom.

The text explains that you also studied subatomic particles, viz., electrons, protons and neutrons, in terms of their discoveries, properties and locations in the atom.

The text explains that you also learnt that atoms with an octet of electrons in their valence shell are stable.

The text explains that atoms can lose, gain or share electrons to achieve an octet of valence electrons.

The text explains that you have also learnt that many properties of the elements are not retained when they form a compound.

The text explains that for example, hydrogen and oxygen are gases, whereas the substance formed when they combine is water, which is a liquid at ordinary temperatures.

The text explains that interestingly, water does not have the same properties as hydrogen or oxygen.

The text explains that hydrogen gas is combustible and oxygen supports combustion, whereas water neither burns nor helps in burning; rather, it extinguishes fire.

The text explains that however, it was found that the mass of the water formed equals to the sum of the masses of the hydrogen and oxygen that combined to form it.

The text explains that let us explore whether the mass remains unchanged during physical and chemical changes. y Water can be obtained from various sources.

The text explains that are all these samples of water chemically identical? y Oxygen is sometimes represented as O and sometimes as O2.

The text explains that what is the difference between these symbols? y Why does dissolved salt in water conduct electricity, but sugar does not?

The text explains that atomic Foundations of Matter 163 Activity 9.1: Let us investigate a physical change 1.

The text explains that place a clean and dry 100 mL beaker on a digital weighing balance.

The text explains that set the balance reading to zero by pressing the tare or reset button.

The text explains that add a spatula full of common salt to the water contained in the beaker.

The text explains that in other words, elements in a compound have definite proportions irrespective of its source.

The text explains that for example, if the water collected from various sources, such as rivers, borewells or the ocean, is purified and analysed.

The text explains that if 9 g of purified water from any source is decomposed, 1 g of hydrogen and 8 g of oxygen are always obtained.

The text explains that in many ancient civilisations, red pigment derived from rocks was widely used in painting and as a colouring agent for various objects.

The text explains that how many electrons and neutrons do they have? y Design and perform an experiment to show and compare that water always contains hydrogen and oxygen in the same ratio, regardless of its source. y Compare atoms and ions of any three elements.

The text explains that (iii) Predict the formula of the compound they would form.

The text explains that assertion (A): Copper sulfate conducts electricity in the molten state but not in the solid state.

The text explains that reason (R): Copper and sulfate ions are fixed in the lattice in molten state, while in solid state they can move freely.

The text explains that choose the correct option: (i) Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.

The text explains that (ii) Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.

The text explains that the species 27Al, 80Br– and 201Hg2+ have 13, 35 and 80 protons, respectively.

The text explains that show the number of electrons before and after ion formation using bar graphs. y Make a card game with cations and anions.

The text explains that possible ideas may include —  Pick cards from the pile or from open cards and match them with cards in your hand to form compounds.

The text explains that you may discard any unwanted cards.  Ask other players for cards and form compounds using the cards they already have. y To learn more about molecules you can explore the link given below —  https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/build-a-molecule/latest/build-a- molecule_all.html Are there any chemical changes that do not obey the Law of Conservation of Mass?

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 9: Force and Laws of Motion

concise_notes.definition: 9.1 Law of Conservation of Mass The Activity 9.2 demonstrates that the total mass remains the same before and after a chemical reaction.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 9.1 Law of Conservation of Mass and 9.2 Law of Constant Proportions as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Force and Laws of Motion; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Force and Laws of Motion through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 9.1 Law of Conservation of Mass and 9.2 Law of Constant Proportions to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
